Canada Unemployment Rate Holds at 7.1% in September — Update
data.description:
By Robb M. Stewart

OTTAWA--A surprise rebound in hiring helped hold Canada's unemployment rate steady in September as the labor market showed signs of life in what has been a tough year.

Employers in the country added 60,400 jobs last month, Statistics Canada said Friday. That was much stronger than the modest 5,000 increase in job numbers economists expected, and it partially retraces the more than 106,000 jobs lost over the previous two months.

The gain helped keep the unemployment rate at 7.1%, which--outside of the height of the pandemic lockdowns--is a level last seen in 2016.

Job gains were concentrated in full-time positions and among core-aged workers, though youth continue to struggle to find jobs. Manufacturing, particularly hard hit by trade tensions and U.S. tariffs, saw its first rise in job numbers since January to partially recoup layoffs this year.

Economists were expecting September's jobless rate to tick up to 7.2%.

When calculated using U.S. Labor Department methodology, Canada's unemployment rate was unchanged at 6%. The key U.S. monthly jobs report has been postponed due to the federal-government shutdown, leaving unclear the state of America's labor market.

The unexpected strength in what are typically volatile job numbers signals some resilience for an economy that has been hit hard by uncertainty over future trade and an isolationist shift by the Trump administration. The Canadian Federation of Small Business's latest monthly survey continued to show that staffing plans are weak, with 18% of employers planning on layoffs and a small 12% share considering hiring full-time workers. Surveys of manufacturers, which have borne the brunt of the fallout from tariffs, suggest little appetite to replace leaving workers and an interest in scaling back staffing at some companies.

Even with strong hiring last month, Canada has recorded little net growth in employment this year--an increase of about 22,000, or 0.1%, from January to September. And while the unemployment rate was steady, it has climbed 0.5 percentage point since the start of 2025.

Statistics Canada's labor-force data showed that full-time employment jumped by 106,100 in September, while part-time jobs fell by 45,600. Still, both are little changed compared with the start of the year.

Manufacturing employment was up by 27,800 during September, partially recouping some of the roughly 58,000 manufacturing jobs lost from January to August. That comes despite weak sentiment in the industry as uncertainty remains high.

Employment for the month was also up in healthcare and social assistance, as well as in agriculture. But job numbers in wholesale, another sector that has taken the brunt of the new tariffs, and retail trade were down sharply following little change in July and August.

Employment showed particular strength among core-aged workers, or those who are 25 to 54 years old, where job numbers climbed 108,900 from recent lows in August. Yet the employment rate among Canadians aged 15 to 24 was steady and the jobless rate edged up to 14.7%--the highest level seen since September 2010--after what has been a difficult summer student job market.

The overall employment rate in Canada, or the proportion of the working-age population that is employed, inched up 0.1 percentage point to 60.6% following declines in July and August. The rate remains half a point lower than the recent peak of 61.1% in January and February.

Employment in the public sector rose by 30,700 from the month before, outpacing a 21,900 gain in the private sector. The ranks of self-employed workers increased by 7,900.

Wage growth was steady for the month, though it continues to outpace annual consumer price inflation that sits below the central bank's 2% target. Wages for permanent employees rose 3.6% on a year earlier, in line with the rise economists had expected.

Total hours worked dipped 0.2% from a month prior, but were up 1.0% compared with a year earlier.

data.title: Safe-haven demand, rate cut bets set gold on course for eighth straight weekly gain
data.description:
Key points:
  • Gold hit a record high of $4,059.05 on Wednesday
  • Silver hovers below record high of $51.22/oz hit on Thursday
  • Platinum, palladium headed for weekly gains

By Anushree Mukherjee and Anmol Choubey

Gold prices rose on Friday and remained on track for an eighth successive weekly gain, buoyed by expectations of a U.S. Federal Reserve rate cut this month, while broader economic and political uncertainty added to its safe-haven appeal.

Spot gold was up 0.3% to $3,985.60 per ounce as of 8:41 am ET (1241 GMT). The metal was on track for a weekly gain of 2.6% so far this week.

U.S. gold futures for December delivery rose 0.7% to $4,001.50.

Non-yielding bullion, which hit a record high of $4,059.05 on Wednesday, is traditionally considered a hedge during times of broader uncertainty.

Geopolitical risks, alongside strong central bank gold buying, exchange-traded funds inflows, U.S. rate cut expectations and economic uncertainties stemming from tariffs, have all contributed to gold's rally.

"It appears that expectations of US Fed rate cuts and investor concern about fiscal sustainability globally are supporting gold prices," Hamad Hussain, climate and commodities economist at Capital Economics.

Minutes from the U.S. Federal Reserve's September meeting revealed policymakers were open to rate cuts to address labour market risks, though inflation concerns persisted.

Investors anticipate two Fed rate cuts of 25 basis points each in October and December. (FEDWATCH)

Markets are closely monitoring risks related to the potential collapse of the French government and the ongoing government shutdown in the United States.

The U.S. dollar fell 0.3%, making greenback-priced bullion cheaper for overseas buyers.

"On balance, there is a risk of a short-term pullback in prices given how quickly gold prices have risen in recent weeks. But over the next couple years, gold prices are likely to grind higher," Hussain added.

Silver is benefiting from the same factors driving gold's rally, alongside concerns about supply deficit and rising demand for the metal.

Silver rose 1.7% to $49.95 per ounce a day after touching a record high of $51.22. It has gained 73% so far this year.

Silver futures on Comex (SIcv1) for December 2025 were trading at $48.25.

  • "Silver’s backwardation is a loud signal — physical demand is crushing paper supply
  • If backwardation holds and physical demand keeps rising, silver breaking and sustaining above $50 is very realistic," said Alex Ebkarian, COO at Allegiance Gold.

Backwardation is when a commodity's spot price is higher than its futures price.

In other metals, platinum was up 0.6% to $1,628.75 and palladium gained 3.8% to $1,464.51. Both these metals were headed for weekly gains.

data.title: Synopsys Receives Final Regulatory Approval to Close Planned Divestitures of Synopsys Optical Solutions Group and Ansys PowerArtist
data.description:
SUNNYVALE, Calif., Oct. 10, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Synopsys, Inc. today announced that it has received approval from all necessary authorities to proceed with the planned, previously-announced divestitures of the Optical Solutions Group and PowerArtist businesses to Keysight Technologies, Inc.

These divestitures were determined by regulatory authorities to be a necessary step related to Synopsys' acquisition of Ansys, which successfully closed on July 17, 2025. Synopsys expects to complete these divestitures on or about October 17, 2025.

The transactions are not material to Synopsys' financials, and terms of the agreements were not disclosed. Synopsys remains committed to a seamless transition for its employees, customers and partners.

About Synopsys

Synopsys, Inc. is the leader in engineering solutions from silicon to systems, enabling customers to rapidly innovate AI-powered products. We deliver industry-leading silicon design, IP, simulation and analysis solutions, and design services. We partner closely with our customers across a wide range of industries to maximize their R&D capability and productivity, powering innovation today that ignites the ingenuity of tomorrow. Learn more at www.synopsys.com.

Levi Strauss Shares Sink on Downbeat Fourth-Quarter Outlook
data.description:
By Connor Hart

Shares of Levi Strauss fell premarket after the company issued a disappointing fourth-quarter outlook.

The stock fell 7.6% to $22.67 in premarket trading Friday. Through Thursday's close, shares are up 42% so far this year.

Chief Financial Officer Harmit Singh said on a call with analysts late Thursday that the company was taking a prudent approach to its outlook due to the complex macroeconomic environment and tariffs, which could affect demand.

The jeans maker expects fourth-quarter revenue to fall approximately 3% from last year, and the company projected adjusted earnings of 36 to 38 cents a share. Analysts polled by FactSet had forecast revenue to decline roughly 7%, and for the company to log adjusted earnings of 41 cents a share.

Stifel analysts said that, while the fourth-quarter adjusted earnings outlook was "a relative disappointment to prevailing estimates," the guide could prove to be conservative given management's noted momentum in quarter-to-date trends.

The fourth-quarter outlook came as Levi Strauss raised its fiscal-year outlook and reported higher sales in the third quarter, boosted by the company's focus on its direct-to-consumer business and broader brand turnaround, which continue to gain traction with shoppers.

Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com

Waller says interview for Fed chair went 'great', not political
data.description:
Key points:
  • Waller among five candidates for Fed chair role
  • Interview focused on economics, not politics, Waller says
  • Trump's interference with Fed raises independence concerns

Federal Reserve Governor Christopher Waller on Friday said his interview with Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to be the next U.S. central bank chief went well and was not at all political.

Waller, seen as top contender to succeed Chair Jerome Powell next year, told CNBC the interview was "all serious economics."

CNBC reported earlier that Waller was among five remaining candidates under consideration to succeed Powell when the current chair's term expires in May.

"I don't know if I am a finalist or not," Waller said. "I thought the interview went well. I didn't get hit by a pitch."

Bessent, who is doing the main job of vetting candidates for President Donald Trump to nominate for the job, has had preliminary interviews with as many as 11 candidates.

But citing unnamed senior Treasury officials CNBC reported that list had been winnowed down to five: Waller, Fed Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman, White House National Economic Council Chair Kevin Hassett, former Fed Governor Kevin Warsh and investment firm BlackRock's chief investment officer for fixed income, Rick Rieder.

Hassett remains the favorite on betting markets Kalshi and Polymarket, with Waller holding the second spot in contracts on both platforms wagering on who Trump will nominate.

The Republican president has demanded the Fed slash interest rates and routinely rails against Powell, whom Trump installed as Fed chair during his first term in the White House but quickly soured on.

"Was actually a great interview," said Waller, who was appointed to the Fed by Trump in 2020. "I mean, it was a lot of discussion about various aspects of talking about various speeches I've given my points of view, they've kind of wanted to follow up on tales of it. I just thought it was great. I mean, it was really there was nothing political about it. There was nothing. It was all serious economic discussion. So that's why I thought it was brilliant."

The question of political interference with the Fed - and its status as an independent body - is a central issue now that Trump has taken the unprecedented step of attempting to fire a sitting Fed governor , Lisa Cook, over allegations of fraudulent applications for a home mortgage.

Cook denies the allegation , no charges have yet been filed, and her legal challenge to his attempt to dismiss her will be argued at the Supreme Court in January.

data.title: Novo Nordisk's Akero Deal Makes Sense But Raises Questions — Market Talk
data.description: Novo Nordisk's minor deal to acquire Akero Therapeutics makes strategic, therapeutic and commercial sense, but Deutsche Bank still views it as mixed. The deal gives Novo Nordisk access to Akero's FGF21 treatment for MASH, a liver disease correlated with obesity and diabetes. Deutsche Bank says Novo Nordisk has labored for years with its own FGF21 asset and, following recent discontinuation, appears to have accepted it needs to go outside the company. This doesn't reflect especially well on internal research and development, it adds. In addition, while small, the deal still represents M&A at a healthy premium, and Novo looks a little late to the party after similar recent deals from GSK/Roche mean the space is relatively crowded, analyst Emmanuel Papadakis writes. Shares fall 0.4%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: T1 Energy Shares Surge on Investment in Solar Cell Facility Developer
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

Shares of T1 Energy rose after the company said it would invest in Talon PV, which is developing a solar cell plant in Texas.

The stock rose 16% to $4.45 in premarket trading after closing Thursday up 48% this year.

T1 said Talon, which is owned by industrial firm Eagle Group, is developing a 4.8-gigawatt solar cell plant in Baytown, Texas, with anticipated commercial availability starting in the first quarter of 2027. T1 is developing its own 5-gigawatt plan in Rockdale, Texas, that will come online in the fourth quarter of 2026.

"The Texas projects are expected to reshore jobs and deliver power to meet rising electrical demand driven by AI, industrialization and residential load growth," T1 said.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: Canada's economy adds 60,400 jobs in September
data.description:
Canada's economy added 60,400 jobs in September and the jobless rate held at 7.1%, Statistics Canada data showed on Friday.

Market reaction:

Link: https://www150.statcan.gc.ca/n1/daily-quotidien/250905/dq250905a-eng.htm?HPA=1&indid=3587-2&indgeo=0

COMMENTARY

DOUG PORTER, CHIEF ECONOMIST, BMO CAPITAL MARKETS

"As much as the headlines were very impressive, there were some cautionary flags here. The number of hours worked actually fell in the month. Let's not quibble here. It was a good report. It's always nice to see a plus sign when the economy is struggling."

"Overall, we would definitely characterize this as a solid and encouraging report."

"We've been leaning to a hold in late October. This somewhat helps that story but it's a close call."

ANDREW KELVIN, HEAD OF CANADIAN AND GLOBAL RATES STRATEGY AT TD SECURITIES

"We would rather see a big uptick in full-time (work) than a big uptick in part time. This reinforces the notion that this was quite a strong number. But I think the overall story here is that this really reinforces what a volatile data series this is."

ANDREW GRANTHAM, SENIOR ECONOMIST, CIBC CAPITAL MARKETS

"Overall, today's data still suggests that a large degree of slack remains within the labour market, which we think justifies a further interest rate cut from the Bank of Canada, although today's strength in employment could delay the timing of that move, particularly if the upcoming CPI print is also stronger than expected."

About RM

RM was founded in 1973, with a mission to improve the educational outcomes of learners worldwide.  More than fifty years on, RM is a trusted global EdTech, digital learning and assessment solution provider, transforming learners, educators, and accreditors to be more productive, resilient, and sustainable.  RM's simple approach enables it to deliver best in class solutions to optimise accreditation outcomes.

RM is focused on delivering a consistently high-quality digital experience, acting as a trusted consultative partner to provide solutions that deliver real impact for learners worldwide. RM's three businesses comprise:

·    Assessment - a global provider of assessment software, supporting exam awarding bodies, universities, and governments worldwide to digitise their assessment delivery.

·    TTS (Technical Teaching Solutions) - an established provider of education resources for early years, primary schools, and secondary schools across the UK and to ministries of education and independent institutions worldwide.

·    Technology - a market-leading advisor and enabler of ICT software, technology and bespoke services to UK schools and colleges.
